section.title: The Angel at Bochim
section.sectionRationale: After the long catalogue of compromise in chapter 1, heaven speaks. The Angel of the LORD comes up from Gilgal, where the conquest began, to Bochim and delivers a covenant lawsuit: I kept My word, you broke yours. The verdict is that the very nations Israel refused to drive out will now remain as thorns and their gods as a snare. The people weep and offer sacrifice, and the place is named Weepers. It is one short, weighty movement, the divine interpretation of the failure just described.
section.thesis: The Angel of the LORD confronts Israel's broken covenant, holding His own faithfulness against their disobedience, and pronounces that their unfinished obedience will become their ongoing snare; the people grieve, yet grief that names the place Weepers is not the same as repentance that turns.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the Angel's covenant indictment and sentence, verses 1 to 3; and the people's weeping and sacrifice at Bochim, verses 4 to 5.

units.label: The Angel's covenant lawsuit
units.note: I kept My word, you broke yours

verses.text: Now the {{malak|angel}} of the LORD went up from Gilgal to Bochim and said, “I brought you up out of Egypt and led you into the land that I had promised to your fathers, and I said, ‘I will never break My {{berith|covenant}} with you,
and you are not to make a covenant with the people of this land, but you shall tear down their altars.’ Yet you have not obeyed My voice. What is this you have done?
So now I tell you that I will not drive out these people before you; they will be thorns in your sides, and their gods will be a snare to you.”

units.label: The people weep at Bochim
units.note: grief that names the place Weepers

verses.text: When the angel of the LORD had spoken these words to all the Israelites, the people lifted up their voices and {{bakah|wept}}.
So they called that place {{bochim|Bochim}} and offered sacrifices there to the LORD.

malak.gloss: Messenger or angel. The Angel of the LORD speaks in the first person as the God who brought Israel out of Egypt and made the covenant, so many readers understand this as a unique appearance of the LORD Himself, a foreshadowing of the Son who reveals the Father. The BSB footnote allows the capitalized Angel.

berith.gloss: Covenant, a binding bond sworn between the LORD and His people. The Angel's speech is a covenant lawsuit: God kept His side, I will never break My covenant with you, while Israel broke theirs by making a covenant with the peoples of the land instead of tearing down their altars.

bakah.gloss: To weep, cry aloud. When the people hear the indictment they lift up their voices and weep. The word captures real sorrow, yet the narrative that follows shows this grief did not become lasting repentance; they wept over the consequences without turning from the sin.

bochim.gloss: Weepers, the name given to the place, from the same root as the verb to weep. Where the conquest had begun at Gilgal, Israel now marks a place of tears; the very naming preaches that the era opens not in triumph but in mourning.

basic.context: This short scene is the hinge between the failures listed in chapter 1 and the cycle described later in chapter 2. The Angel of the LORD travels up from Gilgal, the place where the conquest under Joshua had begun, to a place that will now be called Bochim, Weepers. He speaks like a prosecutor in a covenant court: I kept every promise, but you disobeyed by sparing the Canaanites and leaving their altars standing. Because of that, He says, I will no longer drive these nations out; they will be thorns in your sides and their gods a snare. The people burst into tears and offer sacrifices. The question hanging over the scene is whether their weeping will turn into real, lasting change, and the rest of the book gives a sobering answer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative carrying a strong theological message; it interprets the compromise of chapter 1 as covenant unfaithfulness, not mere military shortfall. We should read the Angel's words as a genuine indictment and a just sentence, and the people's tears honestly, as real emotion that nonetheless stops short of repentance. The passage is not prescribing that we tear down literal altars today; it presses the deeper principle that partial obedience is disobedience, and that God takes His covenant, and its breach, with full seriousness.
basic.originalLanguages: The scene opens with the malak, the Angel or messenger, of the LORD, who speaks in the first person as the God of the exodus and covenant. His speech is a berith, covenant, lawsuit: He kept His bond, Israel broke theirs. When they hear it the people bakah, weep aloud, and the place is named bochim, Weepers, from the same root. The very name of the place becomes a sermon: the era begins in tears.
basic.typology: The Angel of the LORD who comes up to confront and yet still receives sacrifice foreshadows Christ, in whom God draws near to a covenant-breaking people both to indict sin and to provide atonement. Bochim, a place of weeping over sin met by sacrifice, points toward the greater sorrow and the greater sacrifice at the cross, where true tears of repentance find a Lamb who actually takes sin away.
basic.christ: The Angel of the LORD speaks with the authority of God Himself, the God who kept every promise while His people broke theirs. That gap between God's faithfulness and human faithlessness is the whole problem the gospel answers. At Bochim Israel weeps and sacrifices, but the tears do not last and the sacrifices cannot change the heart. Jesus is the faithful covenant partner Israel could never be, and He is the true sacrifice that turns weeping into cleansing. He weeps over hard-hearted cities Himself, and then bears the covenant curse we deserved so that our grief over sin can become godly sorrow that leads to life.
basic.god: God is shown to be perfectly faithful, I will never break My covenant with you, and this makes Israel's unfaithfulness all the more striking. He is patient, coming to explain and to warn rather than to destroy. He is just, allowing the consequences of Israel's own choices, the nations they spared, to become the thorns and snares they will now live with. And He is still approachable in mercy, receiving sacrifice from a weeping people even as He pronounces the hard word.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 5 records the conquest beginning at Gilgal, the very place the Angel now leaves behind. Numbers 33 warned that spared nations would become barbs in Israel's eyes and thorns in their sides. Second Corinthians 7 distinguishes godly sorrow that leads to repentance from worldly sorrow that leads to death. And Hebrews 12 warns against tears that come too late, like Esau's.
basic.oneStory: Bochim is the moment God tells Israel what their compromise means: the unfinished conquest is a broken covenant, and the enemies they kept will now keep them. The people weep but do not truly turn, and the whole book unfolds from this unhealed sorrow. It leaves us longing for the faithful One who keeps the covenant on our behalf and whose sacrifice turns mere weeping into true repentance, Jesus, God come near to save.

context.literary: These five verses stand between the two halves of the book's introduction. Chapter 1 catalogued the failure to drive out the Canaanites; chapter 2 from verse 6 will lay out the recurring cycle of the whole book. Between them the Angel's speech interprets the failure theologically as covenant-breaking. It is shaped as a classic covenant lawsuit: a recital of God's saving acts and promises, the charge of disobedience, a probing question, what is this you have done, and the announced consequence.
context.historical: Gilgal, near Jericho, was Israel's first camp and base after crossing the Jordan under Joshua, the launching point of the conquest and a place of covenant renewal. For the Angel of the LORD to move from Gilgal to Bochim pictures God's presence leaving the place of victory for a place of mourning. Altars belonging to Canaanite gods dotted the land, and God had commanded Israel to demolish them precisely because tolerated worship sites would draw the people into idolatry.
context.canonical: The scene fulfills earlier warnings, in Numbers 33 and Joshua 23, that spared nations would become snares and thorns. The Angel of the LORD who speaks as God appears at key covenant moments across the Old Testament and is understood by many as a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son. Bochim also anticipates a pattern in Scripture where God's people weep, at times in true repentance, at times only in regret; the New Testament sharpens the difference between the two.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interpret Israel's failure to drive out the Canaanites as a breach of covenant, to declare the just consequence that the spared nations will remain as thorns and snares, and to expose the difference between weeping over sin's results and truly repenting of the sin itself.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific covenant confrontation in Israel's history; it does not prescribe altar-smashing for believers now, whose calling is to put sin to death by the Spirit. What endures is the seriousness of God's covenant, the truth that partial obedience is disobedience, and the warning that emotion without turning is not repentance.

debates.question: Who is the Angel of the LORD who speaks here?

views.label: A created angel bearing God's message
views.case: The word malak simply means messenger, and prophets and angels regularly speak God's words in the first person as His mouthpiece; on this view the Angel delivers the LORD's covenant lawsuit as His authorized representative, without being God Himself.

views.label: A unique appearance of the LORD Himself
views.case: The speaker claims to have brought Israel up from Egypt and to have sworn the covenant, and he receives sacrifice, things proper to God alone; the BSB footnote even allows the capitalized Angel, and many read this as a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son who makes God known.

debates.bearing: Either way the words carry full divine authority; and the second reading enriches the scene, showing God Himself drawing near both to indict His people and, at Bochim, to receive their sacrifice, a foretaste of the God who comes near in Christ.

debates.question: Was the weeping at Bochim true repentance?

views.label: Genuine but shallow repentance
views.case: The people are cut to the heart, weep aloud, and offer sacrifices to the LORD, all marks of real conviction; at that moment their sorrow appears sincere, even if it did not endure.

views.label: Regret over consequences, not the sin
views.case: The very next section of the chapter shows the people plunging into the cycle of idolatry with no lasting change; their tears seem to mourn the announced thorns and snares rather than the covenant-breaking that caused them, worldly sorrow rather than godly.

debates.bearing: The distinction is pastorally vital: Scripture calls for sorrow that turns from sin, not merely sorrow that laments its cost, and Bochim stands as a warning against mistaking feeling for repentance.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is dense with covenant vocabulary. It begins with the malak, the Angel or messenger, of the LORD, who does not merely report God's words but speaks them in the first person as the God of the exodus, I brought you up out of Egypt. His whole speech is a berith, covenant, lawsuit, the legal form in which the LORD arraigns His people: He recites His own unbroken faithfulness, I will never break My covenant with you, sets against it their disobedience, you have not obeyed My voice, and pronounces the sentence, the thorns and the snare. When the people hear it they bakah, weep aloud, and they name the place bochim, Weepers, from that same root. The move from Gilgal, the base of conquest, to Bochim, the place of tears, is drawn in the very names: the era that should have advanced in victory instead opens in mourning, and the question of whether that mourning will turn to repentance hangs unanswered over the whole book.

typology.type: The Angel of the LORD who indicts sin yet receives sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: Christ, God drawing near to a covenant-breaking people both to expose sin and to provide the atonement that alone answers it, John 1:18 and Hebrews 9:14.

typology.type: Bochim, weeping over sin met by sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: The godly sorrow of the gospel, where repentance finds a Lamb who truly takes sin away, 2 Corinthians 7:10 and John 1:29.

typology.type: The faithful covenant partner Israel failed to be
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who kept the covenant perfectly on our behalf and bore its curse for us, Galatians 3:13 and Hebrews 8:6.

study.christ: The Angel speaks as the God who kept every promise while His people broke theirs, and that gap is the very wound the gospel heals. At Bochim the people weep and sacrifice, but their tears run dry and their offerings cannot reach the heart. Jesus is the faithful covenant partner Israel could never be: where they broke covenant, He kept it perfectly; where their sacrifices only pictured atonement, He is the Lamb who actually takes sin away. He too wept over hardened cities, longing to gather a rebellious people, and then He bore the covenant curse Himself so that our grief over sin need not end in despair. Because of Him, the weeping of Bochim can become the godly sorrow that leads to life rather than the empty regret that leads only to more of the same.
study.god: God stands before Israel as the perfectly faithful covenant Lord: I will never break My covenant with you. His faithfulness is the bright backdrop against which their unfaithfulness shows dark. He is patient, coming to explain and to warn before He judges, and just, letting the nations Israel chose to spare become the thorns and snare they must now live among, so that their punishment grows out of their own compromise. Yet He remains merciful and approachable, receiving sacrifice from a weeping people even in the hour of the hard word. He is a God who takes covenant with utter seriousness, and who will finally provide the faithfulness His people lack.

crossReferences.note: God warned that if Israel did not drive out the inhabitants, those left would become barbs in their eyes and thorns in their sides, the sentence now pronounced at Bochim.
The conquest and covenant renewal at Gilgal, the very place the Angel of the LORD leaves behind as he comes up to Bochim.
Godly sorrow produces repentance leading to salvation, but worldly sorrow brings death, the distinction that hangs over the weeping at Bochim.
Esau found no place for repentance though he sought it with tears, a sober parallel to grief that laments consequences without turning from sin.

study.oneStory: At Bochim, God tells Israel what their compromise really means: the unfinished conquest is a broken covenant, and the enemies they spared will now become thorns and snares. The people weep but do not truly turn, and the whole tragic book flows from this unhealed sorrow. The scene leaves us longing for the One who keeps the covenant on our behalf and whose sacrifice turns mere weeping into true repentance, Jesus, God drawn near to indict, to atone, and to save.

quiz.q: What is the heart of the Angel of the LORD's message at Bochim?

options.text: God kept His covenant faithfully, but Israel broke theirs by sparing the Canaanites and their altars.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 are a covenant lawsuit: God's faithfulness set against Israel's disobedience.

options.text: God is angry that Israel lost too many battles.
options.note: The issue is not military failure as such but covenant unfaithfulness, disobeying God's voice.

options.text: God announces He is abandoning Israel entirely.
options.note: He pronounces consequences and even receives sacrifice; He does not abandon them outright.

options.text: God praises Israel for completing the conquest.
options.note: The opposite: He indicts them for leaving the conquest unfinished.

quiz.q: What consequence does the Angel pronounce?

options.text: The nations Israel spared will remain as thorns in their sides, and their gods will be a snare.
options.note: Verse 3 states the sentence, fulfilling the earlier warning of Numbers 33.

options.text: Israel will be sent back into slavery in Egypt.
options.note: A return to bondage is not the sentence here; the spared nations becoming thorns and snares is.

options.text: The land will be struck with famine.
options.note: The specific consequence named is the remaining nations and their gods, not famine.

options.text: Nothing at all will change for Israel.
options.note: A real and lasting consequence is announced; the era of thorns and snares begins here.

quiz.q: What does the name Bochim mean, and why does it matter?

options.text: It means Weepers, marking that the era opens in mourning rather than triumph.
options.note: Verse 5 and the footnote give the meaning; the name itself preaches the tragedy.

options.text: It means Victory, celebrating Israel's conquest.
options.note: This inverts the meaning; the name marks tears, not victory, the very point of the scene.

options.text: It is simply an old place name with no significance.
options.note: The narrator draws attention to the name precisely because it carries meaning.

options.text: It means the Canaanites had repented.
options.note: It refers to Israel's weeping, not to any Canaanite repentance.

quiz.q: How should we understand the people's weeping at Bochim?

options.text: As real emotion that stopped short of lasting repentance, since they soon returned to idolatry.
options.note: The following verses show no lasting change, exposing sorrow over consequences rather than sin.

options.text: As deep and genuine repentance that changed the nation.
options.note: The tears were real, but the book immediately shows the change did not last.

options.text: As mere play-acting with no feeling at all.
options.note: The grief was sincere in the moment; the failure was that it did not turn them from sin.

options.text: As unnecessary, since they had done nothing wrong.
options.note: They had broken covenant; the weeping was a response to a real and just indictment.

questions: The Angel held God's faithfulness against Israel's failure. Where has God kept His word to you even where you have not kept yours to Him, and how does that move you?
God said the nations Israel spared would become thorns and snares. What tolerated compromise in your life has quietly become a source of pain you now live with?
The people wept but did not truly turn. How can you tell the difference in your own heart between sorrow over consequences and genuine repentance?
The scene moves from Gilgal, the place of victory, to Bochim, the place of tears. Are there areas where your walk with God has drifted from triumph to mourning, and what would returning look like?
Jesus kept the covenant we could not and became the sacrifice our tears could never provide. How does resting in His faithfulness change the way you come to God with your failures?

translationNotes.note: The angel of the LORD may be rendered the Angel of the LORD, as the BSB footnote allows; the speaker uses the first person of God's own saving acts, which many take to signal a unique appearance of the LORD Himself.
Thorns in your sides and their gods will be a snare echo the earlier warning of Numbers 33:55; the imagery describes tolerated enemies and idols that will wound and entangle Israel from within.
Bochim means Weepers, as the BSB footnote notes; the place is named for the people's response, tying the name to the verb to weep in verse 4.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 2:1-5. No em or en dashes present.
